metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC #2 and AC #8: state explicitly whether consumers that special-case exit codes must be updated (code change) as part of this story's definition of done, or whether enumeration + documentation is sufficient and actual consumer updates are a follow-on; if the former, name the specific files.
findings.finding: AC #2 and AC #8 give conflicting instructions about whether CI/Gerrit consumers must be actively updated (code change) or merely enumerated and confirmed additive-safe (documentation). AC #2 says 'enumerated + confirmed additive-safe with a stated rollback'; AC #8 says 'Gerrit reviewer-bot / CI consumers are updated to recognize 11 before/with the binary ship.' An executor cannot determine whether this story requires opening PRs against CI scripts or just documenting the consumer inventory.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #2 and #8
findings.suggested_fix: In AC #2, append: 'Consumers that special-case exit codes (list the files here) MUST be updated in this same story; additive-safe (any-non-zero-is-failure) consumers require documentation only.' Remove or align the AC #8 'updated to recognize 11' clause to match this stated scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and fix the shape-B close-gate plumbing: `transition_close.py`'s broad `except Exception … from None` destroys the `LLMUnavailableError.outcome` chain before any CLI can read it; the plan must either (a) propagate the original exception (not CommandError) through to the CLI boundary or (b) explicitly extract and re-attach `.outcome` onto the CommandError before re-raising.
findings.finding: The plan asserts shape B for the close-gate path: 'completion-verify FAILS by RAISING `LLMUnavailableError` (`transition_close.py:174`, fail-closed re-raise) — there is NO verdict dict, so its close-gate/CLI CATCHES the exception and reads `getattr(exc, "outcome", None)`'. This is false for the close-gate path: `transition_close.py` line 174 is a broad `except Exception as exc` that immediately re-raises a `CommandError(...) from None`, destroying the exception chain and the `.outcome` attribute. The `CommandError` that reaches any outer caller has no `.outcome`, so `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` returns `None` and the retryable → exit 11 mapping never fires.
findings.location: What section — Exit-code-11 contract, shape B description; `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` lines 174-181
findings.suggested_fix: In `_completion_precheck`, before the `raise CommandError(…) from None`, extract `outcome = getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` from the caught exception and attach it to the `CommandError` instance (`cmd_err.outcome = outcome`), then `raise cmd_err from None`. Update the close-gate CLI/`transition` command to check `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` on the caught `CommandError` and map retryable → exit 11.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `LLMOutcome` survives the `CommandError` wrapping at `transition_close.py:174` so shape B's exit-11 mapping can actually read `outcome.resolution_class` — either pass `.outcome` through on `CommandError`, let `LLMUnavailableError` propagate to the CLI boundary, or thread it as a `CommandError` attribute.
findings.finding: The plan's shape B exit-11 plumbing design is ambiguous because `transition_close.py:174` already wraps `LLMUnavailableError` into a `CommandError(returncode=1)` before the CLI or close-gate caller sees it — the `.outcome` metadata on the original exception is lost at this boundary. The plan says 'the close-gate/CLI CATCHES the exception and reads `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)`', but at the cited line the exception is already a `CommandError` with no `.outcome` attribute. The plan does not specify how the `LLMOutcome` survives across the `CommandError` wrapping (e.g., store it on `CommandError`, let `LLMUnavailableError` propagate past the catch, or thread it separately).
findings.location: What section — 'TWO distinct data-flow shapes' / shape B description; `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` line 174
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add an `outcome` attribute to `CommandError` and set it in the `transition_close.py` catch block (`err = CommandError(..., returncode=1); err.outcome = getattr(exc, 'outcome', None); raise err`), then the CLI reads `getattr(cmd_exc, 'outcome', None)`; or (b) let `LLMUnavailableError` propagate past `transition_close.py` and move the CommandError wrapping to the CLI level. Option (a) is less invasive.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Shape B exit-11 mechanism: either forward `.outcome` from `LLMUnavailableError` onto the `CommandError` raised in `_completion_precheck`, or catch `LLMUnavailableError` directly in the close-gate CLI before `_completion_precheck` wraps it — so the `LLMOutcome.resolution_class` is reachable at the exit-code mapping site.
findings.finding: The `CommandError` re-raise in `_completion_precheck` strips the `.outcome` attribute from the original `LLMUnavailableError`, breaking Shape B's exit-11 mechanism. `transition_close.py` lines 174-181 catch every exception from `llm.verify_completion` and re-raise as `CommandError(…, returncode=1)` with no forwarding of `.outcome`. The plan specifies the close-gate CLI reads `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` off the caught exception — but after `_completion_precheck` the caller only ever sees a `CommandError`, which has no `.outcome`. The classified `LLMOutcome` is irretrievably lost at this seam.
findings.location: What → Exit-code-11 contract / data-flow shape B; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py lines 174-181
findings.suggested_fix: In `_completion_precheck`, after catching the `LLMUnavailableError`, copy its `.outcome` onto the raised `CommandError` (e.g. `cmd_err.outcome = getattr(exc, 'outcome', None); raise cmd_err`) so the close CLI can read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` correctly. Alternatively, have the close CLI intercept `LLMUnavailableError` before `_completion_precheck` re-raises it as `CommandError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact call site and mechanism for the session-log diagnostic write: clarify whether it occurs at the CLI/MCP boundary (after the verdict dict is returned) or inside gate_dispatch, how the current session log is resolved, and that it must not execute inside a gate_read_root snapshot context.
findings.finding: The session-log diagnostic write is under-specified as a mechanism: the plan assigns ownership ('writing the sanitized diagnostic to the session log is owned by THIS story') but does not explain the routing — how the gate dispatch layer knows which session log ticket to write to, at which call site (inside `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` / `_degraded_code_review_verdict`?), and how this avoids coupling `gate_dispatch.py` to the session-log subsystem. Session logs are per-session tickets, not per-reviewed-ticket, so the write requires resolving the current session, which is an I/O side effect that contradicts the in-memory run model (`MemoryRecorder`) used by the gate dispatch.
findings.location: What → Session-log sink ownership

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the MCP failure-shape mechanism: specify whether `resolution_class` rides in a JSON-RPC error's `data` field (preserving the error/result protocol distinction) or a result dict (which changes the success/error contract), and confirm MCP callers are updated accordingly.
findings.finding: The MCP structured-failure-dict return model conflicts with how FastMCP surfaces tool failures today. The plan states 'write-gated MCP gate tools return the failure as a structured dict carrying resolution_class + the class-specific message — not an opaque error string.' But in `_mcp_llm.py`, `review_plan` and `verify_completion` let `LLMError` propagate as an unhandled exception, which FastMCP converts to a JSON-RPC error response (not a result dict). Returning a structured dict on failure requires catching the exception inside the tool body and returning a result — which changes the MCP protocol's error/result distinction for these tools, and may break MCP callers that currently treat a non-error result as always-successful.
findings.location: What → MCP surface (concrete); src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py (review_plan, verify_completion tools)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified MCP structured-failure-dict capability assertion: specify whether degraded gate tool responses are returned as tool-result dicts (isError=false, caller must inspect resolution_class key) or raised exceptions (isError=true, opaque to caller), document how MCP callers detect and branch on the retry disposition, and verify that the chosen approach is supported by the pydantic-ai MCP tool layer.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that write-gated MCP gate tools 'return the failure as a structured dict carrying resolution_class + the class-specific message (mirroring the CLI exit-11 semantics), not an opaque error string — so MCP callers branch on the same disposition.' However, the existing _mcp_llm.py tools raise ValueError (an exception) for disabled/blocked calls and return plain success dicts otherwise. The MCP protocol distinction between a 'success-shaped tool result dict with a resolution_class key' vs 'a tool exception serialized as an MCP error' is load-bearing: an MCP client that sees a tool result dict cannot automatically know to look for resolution_class unless the schema is registered or the contract is otherwise established. This is an unverified capability assertion — the plan does not confirm that pydantic-ai's MCP tool layer (or the MCP spec itself) supports returning a structured failure dict as a tool result rather than raising an exception, or document how callers are expected to distinguish a degraded-but-returned dict from a success dict.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP surface (concrete)' bullet; src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py (all gate tool implementations)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how the shape B handler in transition_close.py is restructured so the LLMUnavailableError's .outcome attribute is accessible at the exit-11 branch: e.g., narrow the broad except, add a typed except LLMUnavailableError arm before the broad catch, or forward .outcome onto CommandError.
findings.finding: The shape B (completion-verify) exit-11 plumbing cannot be implemented as described because transition_close.py:174 already wraps ALL exceptions from verify_completion — including LLMUnavailableError with its .outcome attribute — into a CommandError(returncode=1) via a broad `except Exception` BEFORE the CLI or close-gate can read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)`. The plan specifies what to read but not which existing handler must be restructured to expose the original exception.
findings.location: What section — 'TWO distinct data-flow shapes' / shape B description; Files list — 'src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated `except LLMUnavailableError as exc:` arm BEFORE the broad `except Exception` in _completion_precheck, read `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` there, and re-raise a new CommandError subclass (or set a retryable flag) that the close-gate/CLI can inspect to emit exit 11.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the session-log write protocol: which trigger condition (all degraded verdicts, or only retryable?), which ticket_id the log entry relates_to, how the sanitized diagnostic dict maps to the entry/summary fields, and whether the write is best-effort (errors silenced) or required (errors surface).
findings.finding: The session-log diagnostic write protocol is unspecified: the plan names log_session/append_session_log as the sink and claims ownership, but provides no protocol for when to write (all degraded verdicts? only retryable?), which ticket_id or relates_to to use, what to include in the entry vs summary fields, and whether the write is best-effort or required for the AC.
findings.location: What section — 'Session-log sink ownership'; AC bullet 4 — 'resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic are plumbed through the verdict schemas + coverage + the session log'

findings.finding: The completion gate currently RAISES LLMUnavailableError (not returns a degraded verdict dict), so the CLI's proposed `coverage.resolution_class`-driven exit-11 mapping has no dict to read on the completion-gate failure path unless story 30ff (or this story) explicitly converts that raised exception into a degraded verdict carrying `coverage.resolution_class` before the CLI exit site. The plan says 'the typed failure (story 30ff) sets coverage.resolution_class in the degraded-verdict dict; the CLI reads that key,' but `produce_completion_verdict` calls `runner_sel.preflight()` which raises LLMUnavailableError (gate_dispatch.py line 726), and `_completion_precheck` wraps ALL exceptions as `CommandError(returncode=1)` (transition_close.py line 174-181) — meaning the verdict dict with `resolution_class` never propagates to the CLI. The data flow is broken unless the exception-to-dict conversion happens at or before `_completion_precheck`.
findings.location: What section: 'Exit-code-11 contract'; gate_dispatch.py:produce_completion_verdict (line 726); transition_close.py:_completion_precheck (lines 174-181)
findings.suggested_fix: Have `produce_completion_verdict` return a degraded verdict dict (carrying `coverage.resolution_class`) on LLMUnavailableError — mirroring the plan-review gate's `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` pattern — rather than re-raising. Then `_completion_precheck` reads `coverage.resolution_class` from the returned dict and raises `CommandError(returncode=11)` for retryable classes, preserving the fail-closed contract for non-retryable failures.

findings.finding: The data-flow write site for `coverage.resolution_class` in degraded verdicts is ambiguous. The plan says 'the typed failure (story 30ff) sets `coverage.resolution_class` in the degraded-verdict dict; the CLI reads that key'. But the existing `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` and `_degraded_code_review_verdict` in `gate_dispatch.py` construct the `coverage` dict themselves without any `LLMOutcome` — and `produce_completion_verdict` raises `LLMError` on failure rather than returning a dict. The plan does not specify WHICH layer inserts `resolution_class` into `coverage`: story 30ff's exception classifier, `gate_dispatch`'s degraded-verdict helpers, or a new catch layer in the CLI.
findings.location: What section: 'Exit-code-11 contract — Data flow'; `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` lines 391–414, 443–452, 748–756

findings.finding: The plan says 'the completion close gate in `transition_close.py`' is one of the three CLI exit sites to update with exit-11 mapping, but the close gate's LLM failure path raises `CommandError(returncode=1)` (line 175–181 of `transition_close.py`) which is caught in `transition_cli` and returned as `exc.returncode`. The plan does not specify WHICH `CommandError` raise site(s) in `transition_close.py` should be changed to `returncode=11`, or how to distinguish a retryable from a hard error given that the current broad `except Exception` catch-all collapses all LLM failures into the same `CommandError`. The `resolution_class` field will only be set in the verdict dict when the verifier returns a degraded verdict — but the current code raises before reaching the verdict-reading logic when `produce_completion_verdict` raises `LLMError`.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' — Exit-code-11 contract, 'the completion close gate in `transition_close.py`'; `transition_close.py` lines 151–181

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the shape-A site identification: the code-review gate CLI is `_review_code` (`rebar review-code`), not `_review` (`rebar review`); update the plan to name `_review_code` as the shape-A site for exit-11 wiring and clarify that `rebar review` (ticket-level review_result) is out of scope or a separate case.
findings.finding: The plan describes 'rebar review (code-review)' as a shape-A gate CLI that reads `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')`. However, `rebar review` in `_llm_commands.py` dispatches to `llm.review_ticket` (a ticket-level review returning a `review_result` dict), NOT the code-review gate. The code-review gate CLI is `rebar review-code` (`_review_code`). The plan conflates the two commands under 'rebar review (code-review)' and assigns the wrong shape to it.
findings.location: What section — Exit-code-11 contract: 'rebar review (code-review) read shape (A)'; Files section: '`rebar._cli._llm_commands.py` (exit-code mapping)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the code-review CLI exit-11 data path: confirm whether `rebar review-code` (`_review_code` → `llm.review_code()` → shim) propagates `coverage.resolution_class` to the CLI return value, or specify that the CLI must catch the underlying `LLMUnavailableError` directly (shape B) rather than reading it from the verdict dict (shape A).
findings.finding: The plan names '`rebar review` (code-review)' as a shape-A CLI exit-code site that reads `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')`, but `rebar review` in `_llm_commands.py` calls `llm.review_ticket()` (the multi-reviewer ticket-review flow), not `produce_code_review_verdict`. The four-pass code-review gate is not directly exposed as a `rebar review` subcommand; it is invoked through `rebar review-code` → `llm.review_code()` → a shim → `produce_code_review_verdict`. The plan's exit-code mapping does not describe how `resolution_class` propagates through the `review_code()` shim layer before reaching the CLI exit site.
findings.location: What section — 'Exit-code-11 contract — ALL THREE gate CLIs'; also `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` `_review` (line 68) and `_review_code` (line 129)

findings.finding: The plan names 'the completion close gate in transition_close.py' as a CLI exit site where the exit-11 mapping (reading coverage.resolution_class) must be added, and says 'each with a CLI test'. But transition_close.py has no CLI exit point — its close_ticket function raises CommandError (e.g. at line 175 and 189), which propagates to the `transition` command dispatcher and becomes exit 1. There is no integer return in transition_close.py that a CLI mapping can replace. The exit-11 mapping for the close gate must instead be added in the `transition` CLI dispatcher (rebar._cli or the transition command arm), which the plan does not name.
findings.location: What section — 'Exit-code-11 contract' bullet, 'Sites to update' list; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py

findings.finding: The plan's 'all three gates' exit-code mapping lists only three CLI sites: rebar review-plan (_llm_commands.py), rebar verify-completion (_llm_commands.py), and the completion close gate (transition_close.py). The code-review CLI arm (_review_code in _llm_commands.py:129-184) currently returns exit 0 unconditionally on a non-error result — it has no INDETERMINATE or resolution_class branch. The opening posture section says 'make code-review match plan-review's existing systemic block', implying code-review's exit-code behavior should also change. The plan's exit-site enumeration omits rebar review-code, leaving its exit-code mapping undefined.
findings.location: What > Exit-code-11 contract (Sites to update) / src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:184

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC #5, e.g.: name the specific pytest test(s) that (a) call `sign_manifest` with a degraded verdict dict and assert a specific exception is raised, and (b) assert that the fingerprint re-check occurs before the signing write; or add these as named tests in the Files section.
findings.finding: AC #5 ('the structural never-sign guard rejects signing any degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict; the material fingerprint is re-checked before signing') has no specific proving command or named test. 'Make test green' does not prove rejection behavior exists unless a test specifically asserts that `sign_manifest` raises when called with a degraded verdict.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #5
findings.suggested_fix: e.g. `pytest tests/unit/test_signing.py::test_sign_manifest_rejects_degraded_verdict tests/unit/test_signing.py::test_sign_manifest_rechecks_fingerprint -v` as the proving command in AC #5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test requirement to AC7 for the MCP structured return (e.g., a unit test asserting the dict shape contains `resolution_class` and the class-specific message on a simulated LLM failure), and add the relevant MCP file(s) to the Files list.
findings.finding: The MCP structured-return deliverable ('write-gated MCP gate tools return a structured dict carrying resolution_class + class-specific message') is described concretely in What and mentioned in AC7, but AC7 does not require a test for it — unlike every other deliverable in the plan which specifies test coverage.
findings.location: What section ('MCP surface (concrete)'), AC7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pinned test (or extend an existing session-log test) that asserts a degraded verdict's sanitized `diagnostic` appears in the session log, and reference it in the AC.
findings.finding: The AC for session-log diagnostic write says it is 'implemented' but names no pinned test or observable artifact, making it unverifiable at story close — a weak observability guarantee for a sink this story explicitly claims to own.
findings.location: AC item 4: 'resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic are plumbed through the verdict schemas + coverage + the session log'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit test cases for the `sign_manifest` guard: (a) calling it with a degraded verdict raises the expected error, (b) calling it with a drifted fingerprint raises, (c) calling it with a clean PASS verdict and null `resolution_class` succeeds — these are new failure paths with no current test spec.
findings.finding: The never-sign structural guard (AC5) introduces a new failure path — `sign_manifest` raises when called on a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict — but no test is specified for this path. Only the positive case ('guard is present') is implied; the negative case (signing a degraded verdict raises) and the fingerprint-drift case (fingerprint changed between verify and sign) have no test callout.
findings.location: AC5 / `src/rebar/signing.py` / 'Never-sign structural guard' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the test plan/AC5, add: 'Tests in `tests/unit/test_signing.py` (or equivalent) assert: (a) `sign_manifest(degraded_verdict)` raises `AttestationError` (or the defined exception); (b) `sign_manifest(pass_verdict_with_drifted_fingerprint)` raises; (c) `sign_manifest(clean_pass_verdict)` succeeds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test for the MCP gate tool error-return shape: assert that when a write-gated MCP tool encounters a systemic failure, the returned dict contains `resolution_class` (a valid enum value) and a non-empty class-specific message — this is a new user-facing contract with no current test spec.
findings.finding: The MCP structured error return (AC7 / 'MCP surface' section) is a new user-facing contract — write-gated MCP gate tools now return a structured dict with `resolution_class` + class-specific message instead of an opaque error string — but no test is specified for this new surface. MCP callers are expected to branch on `resolution_class`; without tests the contract shape could regress silently.
findings.location: AC7 / 'MCP surface (concrete)' section / `src/rebar/signing.py` MCP surface via `_mcp_writes.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the session-log diagnostic write: define whether a write failure is (a) raised and blocks the verdict, (b) logged to stderr and swallowed, or (c) retried — and ensure the choice is instrumented so operators can detect a lost diagnostic.
findings.finding: The new session-log write (log_session/append_session_log of sanitized diagnostic) has no specified error handling: if the write fails, the plan does not say whether the failure is swallowed, propagated, or logged separately — leaving a new I/O failure point uninstrumented and potentially silent.
findings.location: What section: 'Session-log sink ownership' paragraph; AC: 'resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic are plumbed through the verdict schemas + coverage + the session log'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'If the session-log write raises an I/O exception, log the exception at WARNING level to stderr (never suppress silently), then continue — a failed diagnostic write must not prevent the degraded verdict from being returned or the exit code from being set correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lookup table or named constants defining the human-readable actionable message string for each of the 8 ResolutionClass values (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) used by the CLI exit-11 path and the MCP structured return.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'per-class actionable messages' and 'a class-specific actionable message' for all 8 ResolutionClass enum values, but no message strings are defined anywhere in the plan or the existing codebase. An implementer must invent all 8 strings without guidance, risking inconsistency across the three gate CLIs and the MCP structured return.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-class actionable messages + exit code 11'; AC bullet 1 — 'a class-specific actionable message'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_CLASS_MESSAGES: dict[ResolutionClass, str]` mapping in failure.py (or a new gate_messages.py) with one canonical string per class, e.g. WAIT_AND_RETRY→'Rate-limited or overloaded — wait and retry', CHANGE_SETTINGS→'Configuration error (credentials/model name) — check your API key and model setting', etc. Reference this map from the CLI exit-11 mapper and the MCP structured-return builder.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Relocate the never-sign guard spec to the existing signing-decision block in `_run_plan_review` (plan_review/__init__.py lines 475-494), where the verdict dict is already available — not inside `signing.sign_manifest`, which has no access to the verdict.
findings.finding: The never-sign structural guard location in the plan does not match the actual signing surface. The plan places the guard inside `sign_manifest` (the primitive in `signing.py`), asserting `verdict.verdict == 'PASS' and verdict.coverage.get('resolution_class') is None`. But `signing.py::sign_manifest` takes `(ticket_id, manifest)` — a pre-parsed list of strings — with no access to a verdict dict at all. The existing guard (checking `verdict.get('verdict') == 'PASS'` and `coverage.llm_ran`) lives at the call site in `plan_review/__init__.py` lines 475-494. Adding a verdict-aware guard inside the primitive requires a breaking interface change to `sign_manifest`.
findings.location: What → Never-sign structural guard; src/rebar/signing.py (sign_manifest) vs. src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py lines 475-494
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the existing `if sign and verdict.get('verdict') == 'PASS' and ...` guard to also check `verdict.get('coverage', {}).get('resolution_class') is None`, keeping the guard co-located with the existing `llm_ran` defense-in-depth check.

findings.finding: The plan locates the never-sign guard at 'the attestation/sign_manifest path' (i.e., signing.sign_manifest), but signing.sign_manifest in signing.py accepts any (ticket_id, manifest) pair with no verdict-level contract; it is also called directly by the `rebar sign` CLI arm (_lib_writes.py:511 → signing.sign_manifest) which never passes a verdict dict. Placing the guard inside signing.sign_manifest would break non-gate signing callers. The guard must instead live in the verdict-producing callers (attest.sign_plan_review in attest.py, and the close path in transition_close.py), but the plan does not name these specific insertion points, leaving the implementation under-specified.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard' bullet; signing.py:sign_manifest (lines 464–520); attest.py:sign_plan_review (line 291); transition_close.py:344–349
findings.suggested_fix: Name the exact insertion points: add the assertion `assert verdict.get('verdict') == 'PASS' and verdict.get('coverage', {}).get('resolution_class') is None` at the top of `attest.sign_plan_review` (before building the manifest) and before `_signing.sign_manifest(...)` in `transition_close.close_ticket` (line ~347).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the never-sign guard belongs at `signing.sign_manifest` (which has no verdict param and 4 unaffected callers) or at the gate-level verdict wrappers where it already effectively exists (`plan_review/__init__.py:475-494`, `transition_close.py:183-211`); if the intent is defense-in-depth at sign_manifest, document the required API change and verify it doesn't burden the 4 non-gate callers.
findings.finding: The plan adds the never-sign structural guard at `signing.sign_manifest` — a generic 6-caller seam (`rebar sign` CLI, MCP sign_manifest, lib_writes, plan_review/attest.py×2, transition_close.py) — but only the gate-level verdict-producing paths need verdict-coverage awareness. Adding a new `verdict`/`coverage` parameter (or side-channel) to `sign_manifest` to implement this guard is premature abstraction: the existing guard in `plan_review/__init__.py` (lines 475–494) already structurally prevents signing a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict by checking `verdict=='PASS'` AND `llm_ran is not False` BEFORE calling `attest.sign_plan_review` → `signing.sign_manifest`. The completion path similarly gatekeeps via the PASS/certifiable check in `transition_close.py` before calling `sign_manifest`. Adding a third, generic-seam guard at `sign_manifest` with no call-site-count justification (Rule-of-Three: 0 of 4 remaining callers — rebar sign CLI, MCP, lib_writes — ever pass a degraded verdict) is premature abstraction that widens the function's API contract unnecessarily.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard (concrete + consumer-safe)'; AC line 5: 'The structural never-sign guard rejects signing any degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Locate the `resolution_class`-aware never-sign check in the existing gate wrappers (`_review_plan` signing block in `plan_review/__init__.py` and the completion-gate certifiable check in `transition_close.py`), not in the generic `sign_manifest`. This adds zero API surface and covers the exact failure modes. Document this as 'guard is in the gate layer (structural by construction)' rather than 'in sign_manifest'.

findings.finding: The sign_manifest guard is specified as 'in the attestation/sign_manifest path', but sign_manifest() in signing.py receives (ticket_id, manifest) — it has no visibility into coverage.resolution_class or the verdict dict. The guard cannot be placed inside sign_manifest as literally described; it must be placed at the caller sites (transition_close.py before calling _signing.sign_manifest, and the plan-review signing wrapper in review_plan/plan_review/__init__.py). The plan does not name these caller sites explicitly for the guard, creating an ambiguity about where the structural check lives.
findings.location: What > Never-sign structural guard / src/rebar/signing.py:sign_manifest

findings.finding: The never-sign structural guard placement is ambiguous: the plan says 'in the attestation/`sign_manifest` path, assert `verdict.verdict == "PASS" and verdict.coverage.get("resolution_class") is None` before signing', but `signing.sign_manifest()` (the named function) receives only `(ticket_id, manifest)` — no verdict dict. The verdict-aware callers are `attest.sign_plan_review()` (plan-review) and `transition_close.py`'s `_completion_precheck` (completion). The guard cannot be placed in `sign_manifest` as stated without a new signature; it must live in one of the callers.
findings.location: What section: 'Never-sign structural guard'; Files: `src/rebar/signing.py` (sign_manifest at line 464), `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` (sign_plan_review at line 291), `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` (line 347)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific call site(s) where the `resolution_class is None` pre-sign assertion must be added: either (a) in `sign_plan_review` in attest.py alongside the existing guards, (b) in `_run_plan_review` in plan_review/__init__.py at the existing sign-gating conditional, and/or (c) in `_completion_precheck`/close path in transition_close.py — and confirm whether signing.sign_manifest itself is modified or left generic.
findings.finding: The never-sign structural guard placement is under-specified: the plan says 'in the attestation/sign_manifest path' but sign_manifest in signing.py is a generic function that does not receive a verdict dict — it receives a pre-built manifest list. The plan does not specify which caller site (sign_plan_review in attest.py, _run_plan_review in plan_review/__init__.py, or the completion close in transition_close.py) must perform the `verdict.verdict == 'PASS' and coverage.get('resolution_class') is None` assertion before calling sign_manifest.
findings.location: What section — 'Never-sign structural guard'; Files list — 'the attestation/sign_manifest path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC #4's schema changes, e.g.: `for f in src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json src/rebar/schemas/code_review_verdict.schema.json src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json; do python -c "import json,sys; s=json.load(open('$f')); assert 'resolution_class' not in s.get('required',[]) and 'resolution_class' in s['properties'].get('coverage',{}).get('properties',{}), f'FAIL {f}'"; done` or equivalent test assertion that exercises the schema's optional field contract.
findings.finding: AC #4 ('resolution_class + sanitized diagnostic are plumbed through the verdict schemas + coverage + the session log') has no concrete proving command for the schema-field additions. `make test` is a universal command and does not prove that the optional fields were correctly added to all three JSON Schema files.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly verifies the runner.py reconciliation: each of the five child seams (exception-classification, transport wiring, timeout/step-cap, output validation, construction-time capability check) is non-overlapping and `make test`/`make typecheck` pass on the reconciled file.
findings.finding: The `runner.py` final shared-seam reconciliation is described as a concrete deliverable in the What section and listed in Files, but no acceptance criterion verifies it was performed correctly.
findings.location: What section ('Integration ownership'), Files list, Acceptance Criteria (all items)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a boundary artifact for the MCP structured-error return shape (e.g., an MCP tool schema update, a section in the consolidated `docs/` entry, or a CLAUDE.md note) so MCP callers have a stable contract for branching on `resolution_class`.
findings.finding: The MCP surface change — gate tools returning a structured dict with `resolution_class` + class-specific message instead of an opaque error string — is a new API contract for MCP callers, but the plan names no MCP tool schema update, MCP capability/return-shape doc, or consumer-facing reference for callers who must branch on `resolution_class`. Without a boundary artifact, MCP consumers have no stable contract to code against.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP surface (concrete)' paragraph; AC bullet 7 ('structured MCP return are implemented')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the consolidated docs (or the exit-11 ADR) that specifies the MCP structured-error dict schema: required keys (`resolution_class`, `message`), the closed enum values, and the mapping to CLI exit codes. Reference this doc from _mcp_writes.py or the MCP tool definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the user-facing error message, exit code (or confirm it reuses an existing code), and recovery action for the pre-sign fingerprint re-check failure path, and add a test asserting that path.
findings.finding: The pre-sign material fingerprint re-check is specified to 'raise' on drift detection, but the plan does not specify the user-facing error message, exit code, or actionable guidance for this failure path — a high-stakes interaction with no non-happy-path UX specified.
findings.location: What > Never-sign structural guard: 'Re-check the material fingerprint immediately before signing ... avoid attesting against drifted state'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a named exception (e.g., `FingerprintDriftError`) that `sign_manifest` raises on drift; map it to exit 1 (or a new sub-code) in the CLI; emit an actionable message ('Material changed since verification — re-run verify before signing'); add an AC bullet and a test for this path.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.finding: Exit code 2 is simultaneously described as 'usage error' and 'INDETERMINATE' within the same What section, creating a contradiction that would send implementers and consumers in two directions. The plan warns '(exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE)' as a parenthetical constraint but then instructs 'every other non-null class -> the existing INDETERMINATE exit (2)' — these two statements directly contradict each other.
findings.location: What section — exit-code-11 contract paragraph; Verified structural facts section
findings.suggested_fix: Designate exit 2 strictly for usage/invocation errors (preserving existing semantics), introduce exit 12 (or reuse exit 2 with explicit redefinition documented in the recorded-decision entry) for non-retryable INDETERMINATE systemic failures, and state the choice explicitly in the exit-11 ADR so the distinction is pinned.

findings.finding: The plan simultaneously states 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE' (in the frozen-contract note) and 'every other non-null class -> the existing INDETERMINATE exit (2)' (in the What section's exit-code mapping). An executor cannot determine whether exit 2 is still the INDETERMINATE slot or is strictly usage-error after this story lands.
findings.location: What section, exit-code-11 contract bullet: 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE' vs 'every other non-null class -> the existing INDETERMINATE exit (2) with that class's message'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single authoritative sentence: 'Exit 2 retains its existing dual role (usage error AND INDETERMINATE/non-retryable systemic failure); exit 11 is the NEW additive code for WAIT_AND_RETRY/RETRY_NOW classes only.' Update the frozen-exit-code parenthetical to match.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that exit 2 is the 'existing INDETERMINATE exit' that other non-retryable resolution classes will map to. But docs/exit-codes.md (the frozen contract) defines exit 2 as 'usage error: an unrecognized CLI --option on a structured read command' — not INDETERMINATE. The current review-plan code's use of exit 2 for INDETERMINATE is itself a pre-existing deviation from the frozen contract, not the documented meaning. The plan must explicitly state it is ALSO fixing the review-plan exit-2 deviation (not just adding exit 11 for retryable classes), or the frozen contract remains violated post-story.
findings.location: What section — 'Exit-code-11 contract' bullet; docs/exit-codes.md lines 14-19; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:383

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the exit-11 rollback plan in the exit-11 ADR to cover the two-phase sequence: (1) revert consumer special-casing first, (2) then revert the binary — or alternatively specify a feature-flag / version-header mechanism so consumers can detect the binary's capability before branching on exit 11.
findings.finding: The exit-11 rollback plan ('revert the exit-11 mapping to the prior INDETERMINATE exit') does not address the consumer-side coordination risk: if CI/Gerrit consumers have already been updated to special-case exit 11 before a binary rollback, reverting the binary leaves consumers branching on a code that no longer appears, silently defeating the retryable-routing semantics without any error signal.
findings.location: What section (Exit-code-11 contract), AC2, AC8
findings.suggested_fix: In the exit-11 ADR, document: rollback phase 1 = revert CI/Gerrit exit-11 handling to treat any-non-zero as failure; rollback phase 2 = revert the binary exit-11 mapping. Confirm phase 1 must precede (or be atomic with) phase 2 to avoid the silent-misrouting window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the exit-2 contradiction explicitly: either (a) clarify that non-retryable non-null classes route to exit 1 (not 2), preserving the frozen 'exit 2 = usage error' contract; or (b) document that review-plan's existing INDETERMINATE-as-2 is a gate-specific exception carried forward and update the frozen-contract note to reflect this.
findings.finding: The plan's exit-code routing for non-retryable non-null ResolutionClass values is internally contradictory: it says 'every other non-null disposition -> the gate's EXISTING INDETERMINATE exit' (which is exit 2 for review-plan, confirmed in code), but also states 'exit 2 = usage error, NOT INDETERMINATE' in the frozen-contract note. An implementer cannot determine which code to use for CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, etc. without resolving this contradiction.
findings.location: What section — 'Exit-code-11 contract' paragraph; 'NOTE on exit 2' sub-note; AC bullet 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the rollback path for the fingerprint re-check gate: either (a) it is guarded by a feature flag that can be disabled at deploy time, or (b) the ADR explicitly states the recheck is intentionally irreversible and justifies why no rollback is needed (e.g., signing against drifted state was always a bug, not a feature).
findings.finding: The plan describes the pre-sign fingerprint re-check as a new behavioral gate ('Re-check the material fingerprint immediately before signing') but provides no rollback path for this addition, even though it introduces a new class of signing failure (state-drift between verify and sign) in a previously-always-succeeding path.
findings.location: What section ('Never-sign structural guard'), AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: emit a structured log event (or increment a counter) at the exit-11 decision site in each gate, including the resolution_class value and gate name, so operators can alert on and measure systemic LLM failure rates.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation (metric, structured log event, or alert) is specified for the new degraded-verdict / exit-11 code path, leaving operators blind to the rate and nature of LLM-driven systemic failures in production.
findings.location: What section, AC item 1: 'All three gates: a systemic failure -> block-but-retryable, exit 11 for retryable classes … unsigned, with a class-specific actionable message'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the guard → fingerprint re-check → sign_manifest sequence executes atomically (e.g., under the existing write lock or with the verdict dict frozen/copied before the sequence begins), and document the atomicity boundary explicitly.
findings.finding: The never-sign guard (assert verdict.verdict=='PASS' and coverage.resolution_class is None) followed by the pre-sign fingerprint re-check followed by the sign operation is a check-then-act sequence that is not described as atomic, leaving a TOCTOU window: a concurrent writer could mutate the verdict dict between the guard check and the actual sign.
findings.location: What section: 'Never-sign structural guard' paragraph; Files: 'src/rebar/signing.py', 'transition_close.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Acquire (or re-enter) the write lock for the guard+recheck+sign sequence as a unit, or snapshot the verdict dict into an immutable copy before the guard and pass that copy to sign_manifest — ensuring no external mutation can slip in between the check and the write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit integration-validation step to the wave-3 entry criteria: before this story merges runner.py reconciliation, require a CI gate or code-review checklist that diffs each wave-2 child's runner.py edits and confirms the named seams are non-overlapping (e.g., a coverage/ownership comment block marking each seam in the file).
findings.finding: The runner.py shared-file reconciliation plan (five children modify 'distinct but adjacent seams') relies on each child naming its seam in its description to prevent merge conflicts, but no integration-validation step is specified to verify non-overlap before wave 3 lands — a merge conflict or silent semantic overlap in this high-traffic shared file has no detection mechanism.
findings.location: What section: 'Integration ownership (shared files)' paragraph; Files: 'src/rebar/llm/runner.py'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify a test for the Shape B non-retryable path: `rebar verify-completion` with an `LLMUnavailableError` whose `outcome.resolution_class` is non-retryable (e.g., `PERMANENT_FAILURE`) exits with the existing INDETERMINATE code (not 11), and a test for the case where `outcome` is None also exits with the existing INDETERMINATE code.
findings.finding: The exit-11 mapping for Shape B (completion-verify) has two branches: retryable classes → exit 11, and non-retryable classes → existing INDETERMINATE exit. The plan calls for 'CLI test' at each site (including `rebar verify-completion`), but no test is explicitly specified for the non-retryable branch in Shape B, which is a changed behavior (previously all LLMUnavailableError → fail-closed exit; now only retryable ones do).
findings.location: 'Exit-code-11 contract' section / `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` Shape B / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'CLI tests for Shape B cover: (i) retryable class → exit 11, (ii) non-retryable class → existing INDETERMINATE exit, (iii) no outcome attached (None) → existing INDETERMINATE exit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` as a verdict-dict consumer (not just an exit-code consumer) and decide whether `_coverage_gap_reason` / `_summarize` should surface `coverage.resolution_class`-specific messages (e.g. 'WAIT_AND_RETRY → retry in a few minutes' vs 'CHANGE_SETTINGS → check API key').
findings.finding: `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` is an unenumerated library-level consumer of `produce_code_review_verdict` that reads `coverage.llm_unavailable` to classify coverage gaps (via `_coverage_gap_reason`, line 77-89) — but the plan only enumerates 'CI scripts' and 'Gerrit reviewer-bot' as EXIT-CODE consumers needing additive-safety confirmation. The adapter calls `produce_code_review_verdict` as a Python library call (no exit code), so it is unaffected by exit 11. However, once `coverage.resolution_class` is written to the degraded verdict's coverage dict, `adapter._coverage_gap_reason` would see the field and ignore it (returning 'llm-unavailable' for ANY LLM failure, regardless of whether it's `WAIT_AND_RETRY` vs `CHANGE_SETTINGS`). This means a 429-driven retryable outage produces the same static 'the review LLM was unavailable' Gerrit message as a misconfigured-key failure, defeating the per-class actionable message goal at the one consumer that directly surfaces text to developers.
findings.location: What section — 'Exit-code-11 contract — ALL THREE gate CLIs'; AC line 2: 'exit-code consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot) enumerated + confirmed additive-safe'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or regression test asserting that non-retryable `resolution_class` values (e.g. `CHANGE_SETTINGS`, `NEEDS_INVESTIGATION`) on all three gates produce the EXISTING INDETERMINATE exit (2 for plan-review), not exit 11.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'plan-review's existing systemic block' posture — implying plan-review already blocks on systemic failure before this story. But the actual posture is INDETERMINATE (not a hard block): `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` returns `verdict='INDETERMINATE'` and `review-plan` exits 2. The plan then correctly says to 'peel off' the retryable subset to exit 11. The internal contradiction is that the plan conflates 'block-but-retryable' with 'INDETERMINATE exit 2', when in fact exit 2 (INDETERMINATE) is the EXISTING behavior for ALL systemic failures including non-retryable ones. The story's new work is only peeling retryable → 11 from the existing INDETERMINATE → 2; non-retryable systemic failures stay at exit 2. This needs explicit AC coverage for the non-retryable INDETERMINATE path staying at exit 2, which is currently only implicit.
findings.location: What section — Two-scope posture; Exit-code-11 contract; AC item 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` explicitly in the consumer list and decide whether its `_coverage_gap_reason` / message vocabulary should be updated to surface `resolution_class` distinctions in Gerrit vote comments.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` is an unenumerated direct consumer of the code-review verdict dict (calls `produce_code_review_verdict` and reads `coverage` fields including `llm_unavailable`). The plan enumerates 'CI scripts, the Gerrit reviewer-bot pipeline' as exit-code consumers, but the adapter is a LIBRARY-level consumer of the verdict dict shape — the plan's new MCP structured-dict return (`resolution_class` in the returned dict) mirrors changes to the same verdict shape the adapter reads. Although the schema change is additive and the adapter's broad `except Exception` wrapper around `produce_code_review_verdict` means it never sees exit 11 from the CLI, the adapter's `_coverage_gap_reason` function reads specific coverage fields and its tag vocabulary is not updated to reflect `resolution_class`-aware routing.
findings.location: What section — exit-code consumers list; `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-command table rows in `docs/exit-codes.md` for `review-plan` (0=PASS, 1=BLOCK, 2=INDETERMINATE-non-retryable, 11=retryable), `review-code` (0=PASS/disabled, 11=retryable INDETERMINATE, existing INDETERMINATE exit for non-retryable), and `verify-completion` (0=PASS, 1=FAIL/error, 11=retryable) alongside the recorded-decision entry for exit 11.
findings.finding: The `docs/exit-codes.md` per-command table has no rows for `rebar review-plan`, `rebar review-code`, or `rebar verify-completion` — the three CLI commands that will emit exit 11. The plan's amendment adds exit 11 via the recorded-decision process but does not specify adding these commands to the per-command table. Without table rows, the frozen single source of truth will document exit 11 in the abstract but won't record what exit codes each LLM gate command actually emits (including the pre-existing exit 2 for review-plan INDETERMINATE, which is also absent from the table).
findings.location: AC line 2 — 'frozen exit-code contract is amended properly: docs/exit-codes.md recorded-decision entry added'; `docs/exit-codes.md` per-command table

findings.finding: `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` is an unenumerated LIBRARY-level consumer of `produce_code_review_verdict` that bridges code-review verdicts to the Gerrit reviewer-bot. It maps any non-PASS verdict (including INDETERMINATE) to a hard BLOCK decision without inspecting `coverage.resolution_class`. After this story adds `resolution_class` to degraded verdicts, the adapter will still convert a `WAIT_AND_RETRY` degraded code-review into a hard `BLOCK — coverage-gap` Gerrit vote, suppressing the retryable semantics the plan intends to surface to CI/Gerrit.
findings.location: Plan section 'What' — Exit-code-11 contract (enumerating consumers); `src/rebar/review_bot/adapter.py` lines 198–220
findings.suggested_fix: Add `resolution_class` handling in `adapter.py`'s `_coverage_gap_reason` (or a new helper) so that a `WAIT_AND_RETRY`/`RETRY_NOW` verdict maps to a distinct 'retryable' tag suffix (e.g., `[LLM-Review: BLOCK — retryable (rate-limit)]`) and is included in the `coverage_gap=True` path, documented in the exit-11 ADR alongside the exit-code consumer enumeration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC #7 into at least three separately verifiable criteria: (a) all four ADRs present at named paths under docs/adr/ (provable by ls/grep); (b) session-log diagnostic write implemented and covered by a named test; (c) structured MCP return carries resolution_class + class-specific message, covered by a named test or jq assertion on sample output; (d) consolidated docs updated (list specific file paths).
findings.finding: AC #7 bundles at least six distinct deliverables (4 ADRs authored, session-log diagnostic write implemented, structured MCP return implemented, consolidated docs updated, make test green, make lint/typecheck green) into a single acceptance criterion with no individual proving commands, making it an epic-of-epics criterion that cannot be evaluated as a unit and that `make test/lint/typecheck green` does not prove.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #7

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge AC2's consumer-enumeration / additive-safety language into AC8 (which adds the deploy-sequencing nuance) and remove the duplicate from AC2, leaving AC2 focused solely on the docs/exit-codes.md recorded-decision entry and test-pin update.
findings.finding: AC2 and AC8 both require exit-code consumer enumeration and additive-safety confirmation, creating a near-duplicate criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, items 2 and 8
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'The frozen exit-code contract is amended: docs/exit-codes.md recorded-decision entry added, tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py updated to pin 11.' AC8 retains the consumer enumeration, additive-safety, sequencing, and rollback language.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the shape-A / shape-B plumbing split (why two shapes exist, when each applies, and how to extend) either as a named section in the exit-11 ADR or as a CLAUDE.md / design-doc entry so future gate authors have a canonical decision record.
findings.finding: The two data-flow shapes for resolution_class plumbing (Shape A: written into coverage dict by degrade helpers, read by CLI from verdict; Shape B: raised LLMUnavailableError caught by CLI, outcome read off exception) are a permanent architectural asymmetry in the gate failure-handling model. This split is described inline in the plan but is NOT captured in any of the four named ADRs — future contributors modifying gate failure handling will rediscover it rather than find it documented.
findings.location: 'Exit-code-11 contract' section — 'TWO distinct data-flow shapes' paragraph; ADR list in AC bullet 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Gate failure-surface shapes' section to the exit-11 ADR: describe shape A (degraded verdict dict) and shape B (raised exception), justify the split (completion-verify has no verdict dict), and state the rule for new gates (prefer shape A unless the gate structurally cannot produce a verdict on failure).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a handoff protocol for ADR content (e.g., each upstream story delivers a draft ADR stub as part of its own AC, and this story only assembles/records), or acknowledge the serialization risk explicitly in the integration-ownership section.
findings.finding: Three of the four ADRs (diagnostic-sanitization, deferred async watchdog, transport-retry side-effect-safety) have content 'authored by noted stories' (30ff, fd1d, 95a5) but recording ownership is exclusively here, landing last — creating a late-stage content dependency that could block story closure if upstream content isn't finalized before this story's review.
findings.location: What > 'ADRs recorded HERE (sole owner; content authored by the noted stories): exit-11 contract; diagnostic-sanitization contract (mechanism in 30ff); deferred async watchdog (content in fd1d); transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture (content in 95a5).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
